title: You will die.
body: Not eventually, not abstractly — you, specifically, will. Stoics asked their students to remember this every morning. The remembering is not morbid; it's clarifying. Most of what feels urgent stops feeling urgent against a true death.
reflectionQuestion: What feels urgent today that wouldn't if you really felt this?

title: The view from above shrinks most problems.
body: Imagine yourself from a thousand metres up. Now from the perspective of someone in 1923. Now from the perspective of you in 30 years. Most current emergencies look different. Some look more important than they did, not less. Both are useful.
reflectionQuestion: What current problem looks different from the view from above?

title: Most of what you own won't last. Most of what you've made will.
body: Objects break, fade, get thrown out. Memories you've made in other people's heads outlast your possessions by decades. The hours you've put into people are the only durable thing you'll ever build.
reflectionQuestion: Whose head are you currently making memories in — and are they the right people?

title: Grief is the receipt of love.
body: You cannot have loved without one day grieving. The grief is not a malfunction; it's the natural shape of love that has lost its object. People who avoid loving to avoid grieving end up with neither, and that is the worst outcome.
reflectionQuestion: What love are you currently afraid to give because of the grief on the other side?

title: What you can't see in yourself is what runs you.
body: Everyone has a shadow — the parts of themselves they don't want to look at. Pretending they don't exist doesn't make them inert. It makes them invisible. The work of self-knowledge is bringing them into the light enough that they stop running you from underneath.
reflectionQuestion: What about yourself have you been avoiding looking at this year?

title: Honest gratitude survives bad days. Toxic positivity doesn't.
body: Pretending everything is fine when it isn't is a form of dishonesty that wears you down. Real gratitude — noticing what's good while also feeling what's hard — is sustainable. It's not the opposite of grief; it's how grief and joy share a life.
reflectionQuestion: What's something genuinely worth being grateful for today, even if today has been hard?

title: On bad days, take the long view.
body: You are not the person you were ten years ago. You will not be the person you are now in ten years. Today's mood, today's setback, today's worst thing — most of them won't survive that timeline. The hard ones that do, you'll have grown to meet differently.
reflectionQuestion: What about today won't matter in ten years?

title: A life you can stand behind is a more useful goal than a life that looks good.
body: Optimising for impressiveness produces a life that's exhausting to perform. Optimising for integrity — being someone you can stand behind when no-one's looking — produces a life you can actually rest inside.
reflectionQuestion: Which of your current commitments are for show, and which are for you?

title: What do you want to be true at the end?
body: Not what you want to have done. What you want to be true. "I loved well." "I told the truth." "I didn't shrink." "I was useful to people I shouldn't have been." Specific, hard, honest. Write yours down.
reflectionQuestion: Write the one sentence you most want to be true at the end of your life.

title: Beginning again is allowed. As often as you need it.
body: Whatever you've been, you're not chained to. The 16-year-old's choice, the 25-year-old's mistake, the 40-year-old's failure — none of them sentence the person you are this morning. Beginning again is not weakness; it's the actual nature of being alive.
reflectionQuestion: Where in your life are you due to begin again, and have you been giving yourself permission?

title: Living well includes knowing it ends.
body: Cultures that hide mortality produce confused, anxious adults. Cultures that face it produce calmer ones. The shadow of the end is not a thing to push away — it's the frame that makes everything inside the frame mean something.
reflectionQuestion: What would you do differently this week if you really let yourself remember the end?

title: The unexamined life is not worth living. The over-examined life is not worth living either.
body: Self-knowledge is a tool, not the destination. The destination is a life — full, present, useful, kind, finite. The work of these 100 lessons is to make that life more available, not to make you a permanent student of yourself.
reflectionQuestion: At the end of this course — what's one thing you want to live, not just understand?
